step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("Polycystic Ovarian Disease")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

I will review my earlier conclusion and incorporate insights from the newly acquired information.
After reviewing the new evidence, there remains substantial support for the assertion that polycystic ovarian disease does not predominantly exhibit increased prolactin. The provided references confirm the characteristic hormonal imbalance in PCOS revolves mainly around elevated luteinizing hormone (LH), which leads to an abnormally high LH-to-follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) ratio due to impaired negative feedback inhibition. Thereby, a hallmark feature distinguishing PCOS is indeed the prominent elevation of LH rather than prolactin. While some cases might experience mild increases in prolactin levels as a secondary issue stemming from disrupted hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal-axis functions, this is neither constant nor integral to diagnosing PCOS compared to the consistent alterations found in LH parameters. Thus, despite occasional incidental observations showing slight rises in serum prolactin measurements unrelated to primary pathophysiology characterising PCOS, we still categorize the latter correctly according to standard criteria emphasizing core endocrinopathies manifested prominently through changes in sex steroids’ dynamics mediated chiefly by excessive LH secretion instead of prolactin modulation.
Therefore,
\[
\boxed{\text{D}}
\] Agree - Elevated prolactin doesn’t define PCOS fundamentally unlike its predominant associations with persistently high LH and altered LH/FSH ratios indicative of disturbed gonadal functioning Tool Call: retrieve("Polycystic Ovarian Disease")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

I will review my earlier conclusion and incorporate insights from the newly acquired information.
After reviewing the new evidence, there remains substantial support for the assertion that polycystic ovarian disease does not predominantly exhibit increased prolactin. The provided references confirm the characteristic hormonal imbalance in PCOS revolves mainly around elevated luteinizing hormone (LH), which leads to an abnormally high LH-to-follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) ratio due to impaired negative feedback inhibition. Thereby, a hallmark feature distinguishing PCOS is indeed the prominent elevation of LH rather than prolactin. While some cases might experience mild increases in prolactin levels as a secondary issue stemming from disrupted hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal-axis functions, this is neither constant nor integral to diagnosing PCOS compared to the consistent alterations found in LH parameters. Thus, despite occasional incidental observations showing slight rises in serum prolactin measurements unrelated to primary pathophysiology characterising PCOS, we still categorize the latter correctly according to standard criteria emphasizing core endocrinopathies manifested prominently through changes in sex steroids’ dynamics mediated chiefly by excessive LH secretion instead of prolactin modulation.
Therefore,
\[
\boxed{\text{D}}
\] Agree - Elevated prolactin doesn’t define PCOS fundamentally unlike its predominant associations with persistently high LH and altered LH/FSH ratios indicative of disturbed gonadal functioning
